data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-08T00:54:19.478Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is a knowledge question about .NET/EF Core evolution. Let me provide what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, there has been a progression toward removing synchronous API support:

- In EF Core 9 (with .NET 9), the Cosmos DB provider started throwing by default when synchronous APIs were used, because internally it was doing sync-over-async which is dangerous (can cause deadlocks). They added an opt-in mechanism to allow it temporarily.


- In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), the sync-over-async opt-in remained available but was discouraged.
- In EF Core 11 (.NET 11), that opt-in would likely be removed entirely, breaking synchronous LINQ operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` with the Cosmos DB provider.

I need to be careful about the timeline here—my knowledge has a cutoff, so I'm working with what I know about the direction these changes were heading rather than confirmed details about .NET 11 specifically.

Looking back at the EF Core Cosmos provider evolution, synchronous APIs were already throwing exceptions by default in EF Core 9, with an opt-in available for sync-over-async. The trajectory suggests that opt-in would eventually disappear entirely. So in .NET 11, operations like `ToList()` and `First()` would no longer work synchronously and would require their async equivalents.
